voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: Central and South America — tropical rainforest canopy
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: Its eyes face forward, giving it sharp depth perception for jumping between trees.
It hears calls from group members hundreds of meters away.
It smells fruit to judge whether it is ripe before picking it.
Its prehensile tail and long fingers feel the strength of each branch.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives in the upper canopy of tropical rainforests.
Large areas of unbroken forest provide the many fruiting trees it needs each day.
It swings arm-over-arm through the canopy using its tail as a fifth limb.
It spends most time in the highest branches where fruit is most abundant.
How does it send messages?: It barks as an alarm call when it spots a predator like an eagle.
A whinny call keeps scattered group members in contact while traveling.
It shakes branches and jumps up and down when approached by a threat.
Each individual has a unique call that others in the group recognize.
What does it do when something changes?: It moves quickly to higher branches away from any ground disturbance.
The group splits into smaller units to forage when food is spread out.
It gathers back into a larger group at major fruiting trees.
It avoids areas near loud sounds or repeated human presence.
How does it learn?: Young monkeys watch adults to learn which fruiting trees are safe to visit.
Infants cling to the mother for over a year while learning the canopy.
Juveniles practice swinging and balancing before they travel independently.
How does it have babies?: A female gives birth to one infant after about seven months of gestation.
The infant clings to the mother's belly for its first few months of life.
Mothers carry, nurse, and teach their young for two to three years.
Young spider monkeys play by swinging and chasing to build skill and strength.
What are its limits?: It cannot survive without large blocks of intact rainforest with many fruiting trees.
Forest fragmentation forces groups into smaller patches with too little food.
It raises only one infant every two to three years, making recovery slow.
Hunting and habitat loss have made it endangered across much of its range.
What does this teach us?: Finding ripe fruit across a large canopy requires memory of many trees.
The group spreads out to find food and comes back together to rest.
A monkey that eats fruit and drops seeds is helping the forest grow.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Uses a fission–fusion society: groups split to forage and rejoin to rest.
Prehensile tail functions as a true fifth limb for feeding and travel.
Requires large, connected blocks of rainforest with many fruiting trees.
Reproduces slowly — one infant every two to three years — making recovery slow.
still_debated: How finely individual recognition is encoded in whinny calls.
How much of the group's movement is led by specific older females vs. consensus.
How well translocated individuals integrate into existing wild groups.
common_failure_modes: Lumping with other spider monkey species (Ateles genus has several).
Describing infant independence too early — mothers carry young well past a year.
Treating fragmented forest as 'enough' habitat when it isn't.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not describe it as a small monkey; it is a large-bodied atelid.
Do not claim it can breed 'annually' — inter-birth interval is multi-year.
Do not conflate with howler or capuchin monkeys in the same range.
